mnemonics.title: Fidelity Types
mnemonics.content: FVC - Functional, Visual, Content (the three pillars of prototype fidelity)

mnemonics.title: Low-Fi First
mnemonics.content: LIFE - Low-IdFea Exploration (start with low-fidelity for exploration)

mnemonics.title: Prototype Decision
mnemonics.content: P.L.A.N - Purpose, Late-stage, Avoid waste, Needs validation (when to use hi-fi)

mnemonics.title: Wireframe Elements
mnemonics.content: W.I.R.E - White space, Information hierarchy, Rules/Navigation, Elements placement

acronyms.expansion: Low-Fidelity - simple sketches/wireframes for early testing
acronyms.usage: Used in early design stages for concept validation and rapid iteration
Used for stakeholder presentations and final user testing
The overall experience of a user when interacting with a product
The visual and interactive elements that users interact with

acronyms.expansion: Prototype Approach for Early Rapid evaluation - uses paper sketches
acronyms.usage: Hand-drawn prototypes for quick feedback before digital design

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a spectrum from a rough sketch (left) to the final app (right). Low-fi is a sketch, medium-fi is a colored wireframe, hi-fi is the actual-looking app. Draw this horizontal line with labels and examples.
Think of a pyramid: base is low-fi (used most, early), middle is medium-fi (some use), top is high-fi (used least, late). The higher you go, the more effort but also more realistic feedback.
Start → Is concept validated? No → Use low-fi. Yes → Is it for stakeholder demo? No → Use medium-fi. Yes → Is design finalized? Yes → Use high-fi. This forms a flowchart shape.

Draw a balance scale: left side shows cost (time, effort, money) increasing from low to high-fi; right side shows value (realistic feedback, stakeholder confidence) also increasing. Show that hi-fi costs more but provides more realistic results.

analogies.analogy: Like architectural models: a cardboard model (low-fi) shows the basic shape and layout, a detailed foam model (medium-fi) shows room arrangements, and a realistic 3D render with textures (high-fi) shows exactly what the finished building looks like.
Like a rough draft or outline of an essay - it gets the main ideas and structure across without worrying about grammar, style, or final formatting.
Like a movie trailer - it gives a near-complete preview of the final product with realistic visuals, sounds, and pacing to generate excitement and feedback.
Like acting out a scene with hand-drawn storyboards - quick, changeable, and focuses on the story flow rather than production quality.
